Iterators are particular, if you're ready to restart it as soon as it fails, it means you basically want it entirely. So you actually want `list(generator())` to finish and be retried if it cannot be completed. So that's you want to retry, which can be summarized by the following example:

```python
import tenacity

s = {'calls': 0}
def gen():
    s['calls'] += 1
    yield 1
    yield 2
    if s['calls'] == 1:
        raise Exception
    yield 3

r = tenacity.Retrying()
ret = r(lambda: list(gen()))
print(ret) # prints [1, 2, 3]
```

So I'm not sure it needs anything else here?

`findsyspy` returns None (making subprocess fail) when running inside a virtual environment whose `orig-prefix.txt` points to the prefix of a homebrew Python, such as:
 
```
/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.11/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7
```

The `findsyspy` function attempts to find the folder where the system python is located by checking for each folder in the $PATH whether it starts with the original prefix; however, this doesn't work in this case, since that prefix is not in the $PATH. Adding `normpath` as suggested above won't fix the issue on OS X.

Unfortunately, I have no idea what the fix should be...
Let me know if I could be of any help.
Thanks.

In my design, a Policy class has a responsibility of suppressing their warnings. But now, ProhibitImplicitScopeVariable cannot know whether an identifier is autoloaded. So, we should export is_autoload_identifier to ProhibitImplicitScopeVariable.

Step1: Export IdentifierClassifier.is_autoload_identifier(node) as ScopePlugin#is_autoload_identifier(node).

Step2: In ProhibitImplicitScopeVariable#is_valid(identifier, lint_context), we can check whether the identifier is an autoload identifier via lint_context['plugins']['scope'].

Step3: Export Linter#_config to Policy via lint_context.

Step4: We can check the desired option "suppress_autoload" in ProhibitImplicitScopeVariable#is_valid.

I'm not sure about the name, either... sleep(0) doesn't really bother me as
a spelling for this operation -- it's pretty idiomatic -- but having an
alias is fine too. If we do have an alias though I feel like the name
should be accurate, and "switch" is somewhat misleading, because this
doesn't actually guarantee that it switches tasks (or at least, it
shouldn't). Really what the operation is is just "let the scheduler know
that it *could* switch here, if it wants". So maybe "allow_switch" or
"schedule" or something would be a better name?

Hmm, looking at the error it seems likely that this is an issue with Zendesk itself. One theory for what is going on might be that one of the Zendesk servers has a misconfigured SSL certificate, and you only hit this server very rarely. That would explain why it takes so long for the error to appear.

If you enable logging we can test this theory as Zenpy dumps the headers on exceptions talking to the API, and Zendesk includes the `X-Zendesk-Origin-Server` header which shows which of their servers handled the request, as well as some other useful info for debugging.

The current behavior definitely does not comply with Python's default handling of mutable objects and is not consistent in itself!

However, I would not say that this is an issue of turning ```dict``` instances into ```Dict```s. The problem lies in the special handling of ```list``` objects. The following example is – as far as Python's data handling is concerned – identical to the above use case:

```python
>>> class Foo: pass
>>> something = {'a': Foo()}
>>> a = Dict(something)
>>> something['a'].child = 42
>>> a.a.child
42
>>> something['a'].child
42
```

In this case, ```a.a``` indeed is a "reference" to ```something['a']```, other than in the ```list``` example given above.

In my personal opinion, lists should not be copied when setting them as a value of a ```Dict``` instance but should be handled the same way as regular mutable objects.

Returning to the original question, I would say that standard ```dict``` instances should be converted to ```Dict```s. In my opinion, this follows the idea of this project, to enable quick and sleek access to a ```dict```s children.

Making this a user's choice – e.g. via a parameter in the ```__init__``` function – is definitely a good compromise, even if this would lead to an increased workload for maintaining this module.

(Just a quick note: While I have the feeling to have some idea about Python's data model and mutable/immutable stuff, the matter is not that trivial. I might be wrong about some of my statements above.)

Interesting discovery, the current [regex to pull relevant objects](https://github.com/tdryer/hangups/blob/master/hangups/client.py#L28) is missing out on four parameters that are probably important:
`ds:35` - important information regarding the current user needed [here](https://github.com/tdryer/hangups/blob/master/hangups/client.py#L221)
`ds:36` - conversation states and names needed [here](https://github.com/tdryer/hangups/blob/master/hangups/client.py#L226)
`ds:37` - user information for all the users needed [here](https://github.com/tdryer/hangups/blob/master/hangups/client.py#L238)
`ds:38` - values possibly relevant to timestamp? needed [here](https://github.com/tdryer/hangups/blob/master/hangups/client.py#L213)

These parameters are in `res.body.decode()`, but the regex pulls them out unintentionally.

I obtained the raw data by pasting this code into [line 192 of client.py](https://github.com/tdryer/hangups/blob/master/hangups/client.py#L192) and reading the resulting output at log.txt.
```
import pprint
with open('log.txt', 'w') as file_:
    file_.write(pprint.pformat(res.body.decode()))
```

That being said, I did some benchmark, and for some reason my version seems less performant (mine was 28 seconds and original was 26 seconds):
```python
import collections
import functools
import operator
import itertools
import timeit

Task = collections.namedtuple('Task', 'id status')

key_func = operator.attrgetter('status')
key_filter = functools.partial(operator.ne, 2)
val_trans = operator.attrgetter('id')


def make_test_list(elements_count=500):
    statuses = [1, 2, 3]
    return list(itertools.islice(map(Task, itertools.count(), itertools.cycle(statuses)), elements_count))


if __name__ == '__main__':
    tasks = make_test_list()
    old_bucketized = {k: [e.id for e in v] for k, v in old_bucketize(tasks, key=key_func).items() if k != 2}

    bucketized = bucketize(
        tasks,
        key=operator.attrgetter('status'),
        value_transform=operator.attrgetter('id'),
        filter_by_key=functools.partial(operator.ne, 2))

    assert sorted(old_bucketized.keys()) == sorted(bucketized.keys())
    for key in bucketized:
        assert tuple(sorted(bucketized[key])) == tuple(sorted(old_bucketized[key]))

    old_bucketized = '{k: [e.id for e in v] for k, v in old_bucketize(tasks, key=key_func).items() if k != 2}'
    new_bucketized = "bucketize(" \
                     "tasks, " \
                     "key=key_func, " \
                     "value_transform=val_trans, " \
                     "filter_by_key=key_filter)"

    setup = 'from __main__ import bucketize, old_bucketize, make_test_list, key_func, key_filter, val_trans;' \
            'import operator, functools; ' \
            'tasks = make_test_list(50)'

    print(min(timeit.repeat(old_bucketized, setup=setup)))
    print(min(timeit.repeat(new_bucketized, setup=setup)))
```

Under [Caveats, "Malformed syntax"](https://jedi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/docs/features.html#caveats) the Jedi docs state:
>It is a tool that wants to help you. But **YOU** have to know Python, not _Jedi_.

From that perspective, I can see it very easily be argued that this is not a feature Jedi should support.

From the perfective of a user of an IDE, it is something I would expect because (for right or for wrong) IDEs get used for discovery by programmers that that don't know a language or don't know the language well.  These developers on seeing "Find usages" shows no usages other than itself may think it is dead code.  Users who know Python are not so likely to make that mistake; however, there is still the disconnect of having to navigate from the definition of `__init__` to the class declaration when a user wants to utilize the IDE for help with navigation.

For a comparable example pertaining to IDEs, in C# if I ask an IDE for usages of a constructor for a class, I would also expect it to find calls to the constructor when called as `super(...)` from the constructor of a subclass even though it has a different name in that case.

Under a refactoring like _rename_ I do not think the relation is relevant to the refactoring. I would not expect tooling to change the class instantiation calls.  I suppose it could issue an error or warning about renaming a constructor, but that seems likely too specialized.

However, for other possible refactorings such as _introduce parameter_, _change signature_, or _replace constructor with factory method_ knowing this association would be important.

commands.bot is discord.client but with a command handler attached. So if I wanted to do your aforementioned kick command I would do 

`bot = commands.Bot(command_prefix='?')

@bot.command(pass_context=True)
@commands.has_permissions(kick_members=True) 
async def kick(ctx, member:discord.Member):
    await bot.kick(member)`

What this does is create an instance of the bot class, and creates a command called "kick" with a context object as it's first arg (contains the message that triggered the command along with some other stuff) and a converter that converts the argument (like in "?kick @someone", @someone is the arg) to a member object. It then kicks them. The `@commands.has_permissions(kick_members=True)` is a decorator called a check that will prevent anyone who doesn't have that permission from running the command.

If you start using the commands ext you *completely remove* discord.Client() and replace it with commands.Bot(), as commands.Bot does everything discord.Client does and more. 

So if you had 
`mybot = discord.Client()`
You would replace with 
`mybot = commands.Bot(prefix=something)`

@NoneGG yes, on python < 3.5 it is just as slow as before. I made some benchmarks which you can find attached to the PR.

If you need < 3.5 support, then you need to depend on `scandir` and to the loop just like in the old code. If you create a pull request that does this I'll close mine.

> But if we need to take monitor on human operation, a hook in file system level is needed, that's why we choose inotify.

I see. I didn't imagine you'd have arbitrary, human-driven modifications. If so you have no other option, short of making sure all those modifications go through a custom application.

> Could you tell me why a huge tree of files is not recommended? Accoding to data i found, inotify is improved with the limit of file descriptor, not like dnotiy.

I didn't say that - a huge tree of files is probably the best way to handle your storage needs. It's the `inotify` that I think is not the right tool for the job, because it's meant to monitor individual files / directories, while what you want is to monitor "everything". Because of its design you first have to visit every existing inode, which will take a lot of time, no matter what. Then you have to set up watchers that will consume memory. And then those watchers will consume cpu cycles at every event. It's true that it's efficient, but you're still dealing with a million-entries hash-table.

Maybe you could approach this from the other direction: monitor for "everything", and filter out the events that you're interested in? The kernel's audit system comes to mind, and it can monitor specific paths. There's also fanotify, but I don't think it fits your requirements.

in the backwards direction:
- the data arriving, will arrive via `gradOutput`
- the result of the layer, will go out via `gradInput`

Forwards:
```
input => output
```
Backwards:
```
gradInput <= gradOutput
```

But actually, there are two backwards calculations:

- `Backward`: calculate `gradInput = backward(input, gradOutput, weights)`, https://github.com/hughperkins/DeepCL/blob/master/src/conv/Backward.h#L28
- `backpropWeights`: `gradWeights = backpropWeights(input, gradOutput)`, https://github.com/hughperkins/DeepCL/blob/master/src/conv/BackpropWeights.h#L27

... so you might need to also check if the nans are appearing for the first time in the weights, during `backpropWeights`.

The driver manager uses the presence of `DRIVER` or `DSN` in the connection string to decide how it will find and load the driver; it is not unreasonable to expect that specifying both may confuse it. Either you use a DSN and do _not_ specify the driver, putting only the information the driver needs in the DSN and connection string, or use DRIVER and specify everything in the connection string and not use a DSN.

After few days of work on PR #50 I am rather unhappy with bunch of unexpected problems (which originate out of awslogs package).

Thas if why following lines are not really focused on pure facts. I am writing them here hoping, someone might give me a hint, which direction to go with better chance to succeed.

## Problems experienced

- installing `mock` package (required for python 2.x) sometime does not install required `pbr` package.
- very strange "half-way installed" status for `pbr`:
  - I was unable to uninstall or install pbr into virtualenv with messages like `Not uninstalling pbr at /home/javl/sandbox/awslogs/github/awslogs/pbr-1.8.1-py2.7.egg, outside environment /home/javl/sandbox/awslogs/github/awslogs/.tox/py27`.
  - trying to install `pbr` by `$ pip install pbr` reported `Requirement already satisfied (use --upgrade to upgrade): pbr in ./pbr-1.8.1-py2.7.egg`
  - following attempt to import `pbr` failed with `ImportError: No module named pbr.`
- https://github.com/testing-cabal/mock/issues/311 is describing the problem I have, `mock` being dependent on `pbr` is causing problems.
- installation of packages and related tests are very sensitive to versions of `pbr` and `mock`. Currently I am not able to find out the pattern to follow to deliver reliable solution - in one commit things are working, in another the tests start failing on problems to import `mock` in Python 2.7 due to missing `pbr`.

## Which way to go? (use or not `pbr`, `mock`...)

When I found the `pbr` package, I was happy to see it and use it in couple of my packages. Not only it strongly simplifies `setup.py`, but it is really used in huge project `openstack` so I was expecting good usability.

With `awslogs` it is the first time combined with `mock`. And I am not sure, where are the origins of the problems. Possible roots of problems might be:
- `mock` having runtime dependency on `pbr`
- `pbr` doing too much magic messing up installed packages
- combination of built time and runtime `pbr` use is causing strange effects

And the options I see as possible direction to go:
- forget about using `pbr` for building `awslogs`, be happy it installs for use with `mock` in Python 2.x
- find alternative to `mock`, which does not mess up with `pbr` at runtim
- wait until someone fixes `pbr` or `mock`

Any ideas welcome.

One important thing to highlight, the code you posted will finish running given enough time. Like I mentioned, I ran your code example on a fairly powerful server so that helped.

The case where the difference will matter is this: suppose the `insert_to_db` makes an HTTP POST request to a throttled service. As `grouped` is currently, it will process `parse` completely then make all the `for_each` requests together potentially hitting your rate limit.

With how `grouped` should be implemented it will be making requests as each row is being processed. Either version of `grouped` will make all the requests, the difference is in the ordering of `parse` and `insert_to_db` at the individual record level. If you don't expect the ordering of those to impact your overall program, then I wouldn't expect to see a speedup once `grouped` is implemented correctly.

Hm, if I had to guess, I'd say that it involves the multiple parallel connections that both sides try to make. Each side sends an encrypted copy of their IP addresses to the other, then they try to connect to all the potential endpoints they just learned. Whichever one connects first is supposed to win, at which point any remaining connections are supposed to be abandoned.

I'm guessing that one of the remaining connections succeeded just after the winning one went through, and the code which handles that isn't correctly ignoring it. The Deferred that represents the connection finishing is called the first time by the cancellation function, then it gets called a second time by the completion callback (which triggers the AlreadyCalledError).

I'd bet this mostly only happens on two hosts that are relatively far away from each other, where both hosts have public IP addresses. (I usually test on two nearby hosts, and on distant hosts but where one of them is behind a NAT box, which is maybe why I haven't seen this error myself).

Like you noticed, it's cosmetic (the transfer still succeeds), but I'd like to fix it properly. I'll see if I can track down the part of the state machine that's failing to forget about the Deferred when the connection gets abandoned. I've been meaning to rewrite the overall Transit state machine (#42).. maybe that'd resolve this bug as a side-effect.

1) You are overriding some concrete methods in the underlying CheckBox - specifically `update `and `process_event`.  While this will work for now, it's not something I would generally encourage.
2) The methods that you have overridden are almost identical to the original versions in CheckBox.

How about merging this all into the original `CheckBox` class and simply providing keyword parameter on that that allows you to specify your list of options on the constructor?  You can default it to `None` and then populate the internal `_options` to `[True, False]` if this keyword is not explicitly set.  That way, you remove all the issues with duplication and concrete method overrides...

## Conclusion

Taking out my own personal opinions about approach to HTML, I think the best thing is to focus on the actual details. I will summarize my argument just on the facts.

I think as things stand now, the suggestion is to remove style in the output, so users can add JavaScript later to add back in the style when the page loads.  One will load instantly, and one requires an extra step of adding JavaScript, and then you must wait for JavaScript to process the elements (even if that time is very small), but in the end, style is directly added to the element in both cases, just at different times. Essentially, the thing we are trying to avoid we end up doing later with a more complicated process for the sake of avoiding lint.

No performance or advantage is gained by using JavaScript, only lint won't fail. Conversely, a disadvantage of decreased page load performance of waiting on JavaScript is added.

One could use CSS  to generate all widths for every possible progress increment, but again, this is an unnecessary requirement for an arbitrary rule which was originally intended to help developers catch difficult to maintain HTML which does not apply to us in the case as we are generating code that does not need to be maintained by hand.

This takes out the arguments on whether a developer should plan for a user to disable JavaScript. Simply looking at what we gain by adding this feature and what we lose, it seems to me that there is no upside. I can't see this approach being widely adopted.

As it stands now, I think I'll provider an example of how to properly use StripHtml, and a simple example of how this could be achieved with CSS.

        results.append(a.tostype('default'))
    for result in results:
        result.wait_to_read()
    end = time.time()
    diff = end - start
    return diff / repeat

def main():
    shape_lhs = (256, 30000)
    shape_rhs = (30000, 30000)
    dns = np.random.uniform(size=shape_lhs)
    mx_dns = mx.nd.array(dns, ctx=mx.gpu())
    mx_dns_cpy = mx_dns.copy()
    for density in [0.01, 0.005, 0.001, 0.0005, 0.0001]:
        csr = scipy.sparse.random(shape_rhs[0], shape_rhs[1], density=density, format = 'csr', dtype=np.float32)
        mx_csr = mx.nd.sparse.csr_matrix((csr.data, csr.indices, csr.indptr), shape=shape_rhs, ctx=mx.gpu())
        mx_csr_dns = mx_csr.tostype('default')
        sparse_cost = 0.0
        dns_cost = 0.0
        mx.nd.waitall()
        #warmup
        check = mx.nd.dot(mx_dns, mx_csr, forward_stype='default')
        check_np = np.dot(dns, mx_csr_dns.asnumpy())
        assert_almost_equal(check.asnumpy(), check_np, atol=1e-5, rtol=1e-4)
        print(check.shape)
        mx.nd.waitall()
        for i in range(50):
            sparse_cost += measure_cost(1, mx.nd.dot, mx_dns, mx_csr, forward_stype='default')
            dns_cost += measure_fallback(1, mx_csr)
            dns_cost += measure_cost(1, mx.nd.dot, mx_dns, mx_csr_dns)
        print("%.2f %%" % (density*100), dns_cost / sparse_cost)

        sparse_cost = 0.0
        dns_cost = 0.0
        check = mx.nd.dot(mx_dns, mx_csr, transpose_b=True, forward_stype='default')
        check_np = np.dot(dns, mx_csr_dns.asnumpy().T)
        assert_almost_equal(check.asnumpy(), check_np, atol=1e-5, rtol=1e-4)
        print(check.shape)
        mx.nd.waitall()
        for i in range(50):
            sparse_cost += measure_cost(1, mx.nd.dot, mx_dns, mx_csr, transpose_b=True, forward_stype='default')
            dns_cost += measure_fallback(1, mx_csr)
            dns_cost += measure_cost(1, mx.nd.dot, mx_dns, mx_csr_dns, transpose_b=True)
        print("%.2f %%" % (density*100), dns_cost / sparse_cost)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()`

Boto3 won't be able to add those imports statements back.  The reason for this is because those inject() calls are specifically done to avoid importing modules until they're needed to speed up start up time.  Adding them back would undo their original purpose.  What you'll need to do is update your setup.py script to include a few additional things.  You can add a `py2exe` section where you can call out specific modules that py2exe missed.  You'll need to add the appropriate boto3 imports there.  Here's an example of where we do something similar for the AWS CLI: https://github.com/aws/aws-cli/blob/develop/setup.py#L60-L66

##################################################################
# Convert a Python double/float into an ES6/V8 compatible string #
##################################################################
def convert2Es6Format(value):
# Convert double/float to str using the native Python formatter
    pyDouble = str(value)
    pySign = ''
    if pyDouble.find('-') == 0:
#
#     Save sign separately, it doesn't have any role in the rest
#
        pySign = '-'
        pyDouble = pyDouble[1:]
    pyExpStr = ''
    pyExpVal = 0
    q = pyDouble.find('e')
    if q > 0:
#
# Grab the exponent and remove it from the number
#
        pyExpStr = pyDouble[q:]
        if pyExpStr[2:3] == '0':
#
# Supress leading zero on exponents
#
            pyExpStr = pyExpStr[0:2] + pyExpStr[3:]
        pyDouble = pyDouble[0:q]
        pyExpVal = int(pyExpStr[1:])
#
# Split number in pyFirst + pyDot + pyLast
#
    pyFirst = pyDouble
    pyDot = ''
    pyLast = ''
    q = pyDouble.find('.')
    if q > 0:
        pyDot = '.'
        pyFirst = pyDouble[0:q]
        pyLast = pyDouble[q + 1:]
#
# Now the string is split into: pySign + pyFirst + pyDot + pyLast + pyExpStr
#
    if pyLast == '0':
#
# Always remove trailing .0
#
        pyDot = ''
        pyLast = ''
    if pyExpVal > 0 and pyExpVal < 21:
#
# Integers are shown as is with up to 21 digits
#
        pyFirst += pyLast
        pyLast = ''
        pyDot = ''
        pyExpStr = ''
        q = pyExpVal - len(pyFirst)
        while q >= 0:
            q -= 1;
            pyFirst += '0'
    elif pyExpVal < 0 and pyExpVal > -7:
#
# Small numbers are shown as 0.etc with e-6 as lower limit
#
        pyLast = pyFirst + pyLast
        pyFirst = '0'
        pyDot = '.'
        pyExpStr = ''
        q = pyExpVal
        while q < -1:
            q += 1;
            pyLast = '0' + pyLast
#
# The resulting sub-strings are concatenated
#
    return pySign + pyFirst + pyDot + pyLast + pyExpStr

8.2.3 Use of the 100 (Continue) Status

The purpose of the 100 (Continue) status (see section 10.1.1) is to allow a client that is sending a request message with a request body to determine if the origin server is willing to accept the request (based on the request headers) before the client sends the request body. In some cases, it might either be inappropriate or highly inefficient for the client to send the body if the server will reject the message without looking at the body.

Requirements for HTTP/1.1 clients:

      - If a client will wait for a 100 (Continue) response before
        sending the request body, it MUST send an Expect request-header
        field (section 14.20) with the "100-continue" expectation.
      - A client MUST NOT send an Expect request-header field (section
        14.20) with the "100-continue" expectation if it does not intend
        to send a request body.

Partially, yes. There are two related things here that are causing this in JS and JSON.

The first is that JavaScript internally uses either UCS-2 or UTF-16 (https://mathiasbynens.be/notes/javascript-encoding). So whenever you use `string.charCodeAt(i)` it will return the surrogate pairs, as that is how they are actually stored in the string. ES6 introduces [`string.codePointAt(i)`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/String/codePointAt) which takes surrogate pairs into account, but I didn't think you wanted to use ES6 here, so as to keep `js-yaml` backwards compatible.

The second issue is that in JSON, astral characters *are supposed to be* encoded as surrogate pairs (see [JSON spec](http://www.ecma-international.org/publications/files/ECMA-ST/ECMA-404.pdf) section "9 String"), as JSON does not have long escape sequences. This does make it confusing to convert between JSON and YAML.

After some offline discussion with @rduplain I think we're approaching something that looks like this (for a really messy topology):

```python
class SuperComplexTopology(Topoology):
    multi_spout1 = MultiSpout.spec(name='multi_spout1', 
                                   streams=[Stream(fields=['foo', 'bar']),
                                            Stream(name='direct',
                                                   fields=['dir1', 'dir2'], 
                                                   direct=True)],
                                   parallelism_hint=4)
    simple_spout = SimpleSpout.spec(parallelism_hint=2)
    batching_bolt = SomeBatchingBolt.spec(inputs=[multi_spout1, simple_spout],
                                      streams=[Stream(['sum'])],
                                      json_conf={'topology.tick.tuple.freq.secs': 1})
    directed_bolt = SomeDirectedBolt.spec(inputs={multi_spout1['direct']: Grouping.direct})
    perl_bolt = ShellBolt(execution_command='perl', script='bolt.pl') \
                .spec(inputs={simple_spout: Grouping.fields('junk')})
    ruby_bolt = ShellBolt(execution_command='ruby', script='bolt.rb') \
                .spec(inputs={simple_spout: Grouping.fields('junk')})
```

where `Stream`, `Topology`, `Grouping`, and `ShellBolt` would be provided by the `streamparse.dsl` package, and `SimpleSpout` is assumed to have the class attribute `output_fields` defined as:

```python
class SimpleSpout(Spout):
    output_fields = ['junk']
```

The idea is that people could define `output_fields` when writing their classes so they don't have to define `streams` when calling `Component.spec`.

It was suggested we could also make `Stream.__init__` take `*fields` before its kwargs so people could do `Stream('foo', 'bar')`, but I'm not completely sold on the idea because `Stream('foo')` looks like a stream *named* "foo" to me, rather than a stream called "default" with one output field called "foo".

Right, you can do this with a bolt and streams (meant to link to this https://streamparse.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#streamparse.bolt.Bolt.emit). Looks like there isn't a good example in the docs. I'll try to make one.

Also, ignore the `python3.5` error, it behaves like that because of [this](https://github.com/kivy/python-for-android/blob/084ddec7ff888fbc760b1f20280864f7b6bf02e5/pythonforandroid/bootstraps/sdl2/build/src/org/kivy/android/PythonUtil.java#L28) (the for loop) and therefore it tries to load everything from the loop and prints what it couldn't. To be more clear, when it's a `python` what should be loaded, it tries to load the py2 first (I think) and then it'll just enable a switch with `skippedPython = true;` to ignore `python3.5`.

Why you see the py3.5 error is basically because py2 wasn't loaded. And it wasn't loaded because something's wrong with your `sqlite3`. Does it have the right permissions in the `app-lib` folder? Perhaps a intermediate approach would be to simply allow the ast parser to recognize `numpy.` attributes as potential function calls, so the user doesn't have to bother with dropping the module handles when switching from NumPy prototype to NumExpr.  It would cost ~20 us of extra parse time per `numpy.` attribute access.

This could be further extended to allow mapping to the underlying math libraries (once we get there) to allow mixing libraries within a single statement.

E.g.

    `erf(...)*numpy.sin(...)`

versus

    `erf(...)*yep.sin(...)`

or 

    `erf(...)*mkl.sin(...)`

We could make the format evolve just enough to fit these metadata.

However, after spending some time working on parameters in different context (implementing fancy indexing, building a web client that renders table of parameters), I am convinced that scales is a _legislative concept_ that is right now implemented  _way too deep_  in OpenFisca.

Some arguments:

- Each time we are doing some works on parameters, we often have to do it twice to handle scales.
- This doesn't scale (no pun intended): we handle 3 types of scales, but there may be other legitimate common calculation patterns to share. Should we keep complexify our parameter  taxonomy?
- This makes `P = tax_benefit_system.parameters(2018)` a very inhomogeneous object:
   - `P.taxes.housing_tax.rate` is a float
   - `P.taxes.housing_tax` is basically a dict wrapper, which keys are `rate` and `minimal_amount`. Makes sense so far.
   - `P.taxes.social_security_contribution` is a `MarginalRateTaxScale`, a complex object that does very domain-specific calculations. This feels clumsy.

I feel like we should ideally move towards a different approach, already mentioned in https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-core/issues/657#issuecomment-404001064:
- Keep parameters really simple. They are just data structured in a tree.
- On top of that, build reusable patterns. These patterns may be provided by Core, or built by users, but they should be a separate layer.

Now, this whole reflexion goes far beyond this issue, but I believe that if we want to go in that direction, and if we are to publish a breaking change on the scale route anyway, it's a good place to start.

I implemented a separate case in `_save_one` and `_load_one` for the `BiRNNBuilder`, but I think it might be more elegant to make it extend the `Saveable` interface instead. The problem with this is that the builder layer sub-components are saved in a list of tuples rather than directly as attributes, and `Saveable` assumes that any non-picklable sub-component is a direct attribute of the object.
A possible way to go around this is to make replace the above assumption with a decorator that would declare an attribute as non-picklable (explicit is better than implicit), and add this decorator to the `builder_layers` attribute.

One quick note about the system test - I think it's fine but in general the gotcha to be aware of with Logging is that you can get an empty page followed by a page with results. The nextPageToken is used as a sort of "pending" sign (not a fan of this but that's how it works). So in theory `entry = next(iter)` could fail if the query is too slow, really you always want to wrap it it in a loop (not sure if there is an elegant way to have client library do this for you). Now usually you only get slow queries when looking through huge amounts of logs like all of the ones for a project but it's something to be aware of. Again not really an issue just giving you a heads up on something that's burned me and others.

Gabe, thanks for the response. To be honest I tried following the logic in Github between suds and the various googleads classes and got a bit turned around*. Rather than proposing a solution at this point (we may get to that later) I want to simply clarify the problem: while our socket timeout is adequate, the absence of a keep-alive header forces the NAT gateway to drop the connection after 350 seconds. As such we would want the ability to add the keep-alive headers to _any_ connection we find timing out. To date that's happening while asking for a specific report to be generated, and for the download of said report.

*One thought that did occur to me was that perhaps the change in code could happen on the suds code itself in https://github.com/googleads/googleads-python-lib/blob/master/googleads/util.py rather than in the common.py codebase. If we do end up having to monkeypatch the library, that may be the approach we take, but we'd obviously prefer a clean solution.

The subtask functionality is all deprecated. (Hence the notes about not being available in the future.)
We can just strike those from the list.

---

Task `expansions` and Endpoint `credential_subject` are both simple fields.
If they're added to the API, we'll add CLI support, of course. Not much to say about those.

---

`--myproxy-lifetime` is very straightforward. We should just knock that out.

---

`globus endpoint server delete` by hostname is a little fiddly and not important enough to consider blocking.
We'd have various specialized cases to handle (multiple matches, no matches, etc), and users can already `endpoint server list` followed by `endpoint server delete`. It's not hard, but unless someone comes to us with a need for it, it's unnecessary complexity.

If someone were scripting endpoint server deletion by hostname, that would be legitimate, but I haven't heard of anyone doing that.

---

`globus task wait` can't show a progress bar. We don't have the necessary information to render one. I doubt that the one shown by the hosted CLI is accurate, or it may be based on information not exposed through the API.

Files completed and mb/s , both of which can be seen with a simple `globus task show`, are not important enough to disrupt the currently simple/clean output.
Printing `.` to indicate that the wait is still polling (and that the command didn't hang somewhere) is good and useful.

The problem here seems to be related to mixed-dtype DataFrames not having a single backing NumPy array. In this case `.values` returns a copy of the DataFrame with the most common dtype. If the DataFrame is of a single dtype, then all columns are backed by a single Matrix. This matrix is then returned as the result of `.values`. Only in the latter case, modifications to the matrix returned by `.values` will be back-propagated to the original DataFrame.

This issue should be fixed.  The relevant fixed packages were:

python-2.7.10-4.
python-3.4.3-4
python-3.5.0-3

conda-env-2.4.4 (build 2)
menuinst-1.2.2

The fix is that the conda-env post-link script has been removed.  It has been replaced by a modified Lib/site.py file in each Python installation, plus a library_bin.pth file in each Python installation.  The combination of these lead to Library/bin being added to os.environ["PATH"] for any Python process.  Any programs you install that end up in Library/bin (for example, patch) will still not be on PATH for older installations.  You can either modify PATH to add Library/bin yourself, or uninstall and reinstall using a new installer (Anaconda >= 2.4.0; Miniconda >= 3.18)

FWIW when using BLAS I've observed worse performance from allowing multiple hierarchies of concurrency.  If you call many matrix multiplies with multiple GIL releasing Python threads, and if each of those threads calls a multi-core BLAS then all of the threads have crazy contention and reduce performance significantly.  The table at the top of [this post](http://matthewrocklin.com/blog/work/2015/01/14/Towards-OOC-MatMul/) shows the performance loss.

Of course, blosc operation and matrix-multiply are quite different computationally.  Blosc probably thrashes memory less so this is less of an issue.  Just wanted to point out the counter-example that sometimes you don't want to boundlessly increase parallelism.

`pre-commit` will only install things into places it manages: the isolated virtualenvs in `~/.cache/pre-commit`.  For pylint, this would require `language: python`.  Since pylint does dynamic analysis (it needs to effectively _import_ your code to properly lint it) it's often better to manage `pylint` outside of `pre-commit` and use a `local` hook.

@minrk Looking back at the sources from the traceback, here are some thoughts. Since somewhere along the line when the program gets to write_message, the system has either prematurely closed a websocket or thinks the websocket is closed so an error is thrown.

Here are a couple of PRs that may be related:
- [747](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/pull/747/files)

- [898](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/pull/898/files)
  In this PR, `stream` is set to `None` in `WebSocketMixin` class. When we get further down to where one of the traceback issues was found in [`zmqhandlers` file and `ZMQStreamHandler`](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/f19d68a33193dff07e62f11da0ebce331e8acbda/notebook/base/zmqhandlers.py#L212), it's possible that `stream` is still None when `ZMQStreamHandler` is used which may cause the error handler to be called and the traceback emitted. My gut instinct is this is where the problem is, but I don't know the code base well enough to be sure.

Alternatively, this could be context issue where we are not handling the context correctly in eventloop.
```
# From tornado/stack_context.py
`StackContext` shifts the burden of restoring that state
from each call site (e.g.  wrapping each `.AsyncHTTPClient` callback
in ``async_callback``) to the mechanisms that transfer control from
one context to another (e.g. `.AsyncHTTPClient` itself, `.IOLoop`,
thread pools, etc).
...
Most applications shouldn't have to work with `StackContext` directly.
Here are a few rules of thumb for when it's necessary:
* If you're writing an asynchronous library that doesn't rely on a
  stack_context-aware library like `tornado.ioloop` or `tornado.iostream`
  (for example, if you're writing a thread pool), use
  `.stack_context.wrap()` before any asynchronous operations to capture the
  stack context from where the operation was started.
* If you're writing an asynchronous library that has some shared
  resources (such as a connection pool), create those shared resources
  within a ``with stack_context.NullContext():`` block.  This will prevent
  ``StackContexts`` from leaking from one request to another.
* If you want to write something like an exception handler that will
  persist across asynchronous calls, create a new `StackContext` (or
  `ExceptionStackContext`), and make your asynchronous calls in a ``with``
  block that references your `StackContext`.
```

@mreiferson For consistency you'll also want to remove https://github.com/nsqio/pynsq/blob/master/nsq/async.py#L489.

I don't think the change you suggest is enough on its own.  I was a little cryptic with the above diff and didn't describe the way this problem interacts with the `RDY` redistribution logic.  There are two interlocking issues here.  One is what you mention: the semantics of `RDY` count are currently different on the server and client.  With the change you suggest they're brought into agreement and so no need to use `last_rdy` as in my diff.

The second issue is the need for redistribution of `RDY` count when max-in-flight is less than the connection count.  With the change you suggest, this  https://github.com/nsqio/pynsq/blob/master/nsq/reader.py#L664 becomes the only route outside of backoff by which `total_rdy` can decrease.  So you're going to have`max_in_flight == total_rdy` as long as connections with `RDY` greater than 0 are receiving messages more frequently than `low_rdy_idle_timeout`.  This means that unless and until the currently active connections are drained and hit the timeout, both paths to redistribution are blocked:

1. This quantity https://github.com/nsqio/pynsq/blob/master/nsq/reader.py#L671 is going be 0.
2. A connection selected in this block for update https://github.com/nsqio/pynsq/blob/master/nsq/reader.py#L323 will be blocked in `_send_rdy()` and scheduled for retry (https://github.com/nsqio/pynsq/blob/master/nsq/reader.py#L460), but retries won't succeed until something decrements `total_rdy`.

If you do want to guarantee progress on all connections then you need to redistribute `RDY` away from active connections more aggressively, which means setting `RDY == 0` on some connections even though they're still receiving messages.  But in that case you need to be careful not to send `RDY 1` while the connections that are losing `RDY` still have messages in flight, or else the resulting race condition opens you up to a max-in-flight violation.  Hence you still need to be looking at the sum of `max(conn.rdy, conn.in_flight)` over all connections, not just the sum of `conn.rdy`.

> (It feels a bit like I'm being contrary to be contrary; but I'm really trying not to be!)

No worries! It's good that new ideas get discussed and questioned before they are implemented.

> I wonder if flag dependencies is a bit like inheritance in OOP based languages; something that many languages are trying to get away from? e.g. traits in Rust, interfaces in Go, and the lack of inheritance in the original SmallTalk definitions.

> By this, I mean, when using a dependent flag, part of its functionality is hidden via the dependency mechanism?

It's an interesting thought and they are definitely related but I would argue no. There is a similarity in that both the inherited and the required entity need to exist before the child or dependent entity.  However, the big difference is that an inheritance imports functionality into the child making the child extended, while a dependency simply creates an order between entities.

Sure, you could argue that having a dependencies would let you assume things about the environment (for example you know that a file exist because you depend on it's creation), but I really can't see how that would translate to _hidden_ functionality.

> Objects attached to a flag is something I've been wanting for a while to send information from one layer to the next.

That's an interesting approach. I like the idea of being able to communicate between layers in a common way. Not sure if attaching objects to flags is the best approach though. In my opinion flags should only be concerned about activating handlers, not return relation data or incorporate something that is not directly tied to the state of the charm.

If you want to share some information that doesn't fit well in the unit key-value store I would probably just expose it in the base layer module and import it in the upper layer.

reactive/base.py
```python
some_object = None

@when('installed')
def setup_model():
    some_object = SomeObject()
    add_flag('base.ready')
```

reactive/app.py
```python
from . import base

@when('base.ready')
def start():
    do_something(base.some_object)
```

But if someone have some idea of how to make that kind of sharing more standardized I'm all for it!

In addition to verifying that each entity method maps to an appropriate underlying API path, the arguments to each entity method should be verified. How so? An an example, take a look at these two method signatures:

    def add_subscriptions(self, params):
    def content_override(self, content_label, value):

The first method accepts a single parameter, `params`. `params` is a dict that is inserted directly into an HTTP PUT call:

```python
response = client.put(
    self.path('add_subscriptions'),
    params,
    auth=self._server_config.auth,
    verify=self._server_config.verify,
)
```

The second method accepts two parameters. These parameters are placed into an HTTP PUT call one-by-one:

```python
response = client.put(
    self.path('content_override'),
    {'content_override': {
        'content_label': content_label,
        'value': value,
    }},
    auth=self._server_config.auth,
    verify=self._server_config.verify,
)
```

Which strategy is better? I think that the latter strategy is sinificantly better. One of the aims of NailGun is to shield developers from the idiosyncracies of the API, and one of those idiosyncracies is the uneven usage of nested dicts. The latter method allows a developer to pass in information and now worry about the layout of the JSON payload. In addition, the latter method allows each individual parameter to be documented individually. This means that a dev can read relevant method documentation at http://nailgun.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/nailgun.html#module-nailgun.entities, instead of being forced to trawl through the API documentation that ships with Satellite.

What's the take-away? In addition to reviewing method placement and naming, method arguments should also be reviewed.

After closer look I came to conclusion that we rather need to improve URL field validation for the local URLs, than promotions loading.

If we have promotions for pages `/` and `/de/` (in English and German languages), there are few scenarios how they can be loaded:

- Load using locale-neutral page URL (`/`) will load always _only_ English content block.
- Load using both locale-neutral and locale-specific URLs will load content in _both_ languages, which might be not relevant for the customers.
- Load firstly using locale-specific URL (`/de/`) and if no relevant records, fallback to local-neutral URL (`/`). In this case, it will work as initially intended, but we won't have control where we want to display content block and where not and just display everywhere the same content block.

We already can attach content block to any number of pages, but the URL validator respects only current locale, so that you can attach promotion to `/en-gb/` page on default locale and not `/de/` and visa versa, which is the main issue, I believe.

> If your operating system includes a packaged copy of the `letsencrypt` client, install it from there and use the letsencrypt command. Otherwise, you can use our `letsencrypt-auto` wrapper script to get a copy quickly:
>
> $ git clone https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt
> $ cd letsencrypt
> $ ./letsencrypt-auto --help
>
> `letsencrypt-auto` accepts the same flags as `letsencrypt`; it installs all of its own dependencies and updates the client code automatically (but it’s comparatively slow and large in order to achieve that).

Basically, there are two options for clients: `letsencrypt` (a "native client" operating system package, which may or may not be available on your particular platform) and `letsencrypt-auto` (a downloadable, "all inclusive", python-based client). They function identically. The documentation had to choose one or the other command, and they chose to use `letsencrypt`.

You're getting the `-bash: ./letsencrypt: Is a directory` because you didn't `cd letsencrypt` before trying to run the command (and `letsencrypt` is the name of the directly created by cloning the repo).

@tim-one I've created an issue (#228) to further explore the `absolutedelta` concept, but I think it's very reasonable considering we already have a `relativedelta` object. I think this is the right solution for this library, since we don't have any backwards-compatibility issues, and it allows for the behavior to be done explicitly if so desired. I suspect that for the most part, people will be perfectly happy to use either `timedelta` or `relativedelta`. I don't think it's the job of `tzinfo` objects to specify how arithmetic operations occur, they should just be providing information about the time zone so that you *can* get the behavior you want.

As for the subtraction situation, there's already precedent in the `relativedelta` object - `relativedelta`s can be constructed either by explicitly specifying the calendar date offset *or* from two `datetime` objects.

How would a "file" object handle large files? In enabling encryption would you need access to the entire file? The use case for B2Fuse of reading parts of files is to avoid having to download the entire file before starting to return data to user applications. If you then have to download the entire file to make encryption work, that would bring us back to square one. 

File like objects present a clear and concise way of interacting with data you provide. While at the same time I am unsure if it should be Python B2 API's job to handle the allocation of memory or disk space to store these files. Would it not make more sense that Python B2 API tried to be as minimal, fast and transparent as possible? Maybe this is a non-issue if you don't need to download the entire file to decrypt. I had a similar problem and solved it using the [Function](https://marshmallow.readthedocs.io/en/3.0/api_reference.html#marshmallow.fields.Function) field type and creating appropriate serialisation/deserialisation methods which checked the types.  Thought it worth mentioning in case it helps anyone - it's simpler than making your own field subclass.

> If value is a float, the binary floating point value is losslessly converted to its exact decimal equivalent. This conversion can often require 53 or more digits of precision. For example, Decimal(float('1.1')) converts to Decimal('1.100000000000000088817841970012523233890533447265625').

https://docs.python.org/2/library/decimal.html#decimal.Decimal

Since there is no way to distinguish between `1.1` and `1.100000000000000088817841970012523233890533447265625` the safest thing to do is preserve the precision.

It appears that a the `__str__` implementation for the builtin `float` type truncates the value to 12 significant digits.

```py
str(1.10000000001)
# '1.10000000001'
str(1.100000000001)
# '1.1'
str(0.0000000000123456789012)
# '1.23456789012e-11'
str(0.00000000001234567890123)
# '1.23456789012e-11'
```

This seems like a potentially destructive transformation. Maybe it would be better to keep the existing behavior, document the existing behavior, and advise against passing decimal values as float literals.

On the other hand, since the built in float type can only guarantee about 12 digits of precision, it seems like a reasonable compromise to truncate the ambiguous precision.

The only use case I am concerned about is a schema that a developer may not have control over, but must deserialize with marshmallow. If a decimal value requires more that 12 digits of precision, but is supplied as a float literal, this truncation is almost guaranteed to introduce more error into the value than the error produced by the underlying binary representation.

This change seems to compromise accuracy of all values in favor of the perfection of an arbitrary subset of values.

A more surgical solution would be to inspect the original data provided to `loads()` in order to construct an exact `Decimal` from a string. There seems to be no hope for recovering `float` literals provided to `load()`, so preserving the existing precision seems like the correct behavior.

> Another way to do this would be to add code to cuda_convnet.py and dnn.py which replaces those Layer classes with dummy wrappers around Conv2DLayer when the libraries are unavailable.

Yeah, that would work.  I can think of a few ways to do this with minimal overhead which may or may not be best - e.g., defining a function `Conv2DLayer` which returns an instance of the correct underlying class given the value of a new `backend` keyword argument, with correct fallbacks.  It would look like a class constructor to the end-user.  Here's an incomplete example of what I mean:

```Python
from theano.sandbox.cuda import dnn
if not theano.config.device.startswith("gpu") or not dnn.dnn_available():
    HAS_DNN = True
else:
    HAS_DNN = False

def Conv2DLayer(incoming, num_filters, filter_size, backend='theano', **kwargs):
    if backend == 'cudnn':
        if HAS_DNN:
            return Conv2DDNNLayer(incoming, num_filters, filter_size, **kwargs)
        else:
            warnings.warn("backend='dnn' was provided, but cudnn is not available.")
            backend = 'theano'
    if backend == 'theano':
            # We'd need to rename the current Conv2DLayer, this is a bad name
            return Conv2DTheanoLayer(incoming, num_filters, filter_size, **kwargs)
```
etc.

> One complication is that Conv2DLayer doesn't support pad at the moment.

and

> the problem is that these layer implementations are not always interchangeable. They implement a different subset of features. 

Right, but I think it's definitely preferable to have them just throw errors when there is a feature that is requested but not available (padding, or non-square filters which I believe aren't supported by `cuda_convnet`, etc.)  Ideally, we'd have a backend which works for all kwargs all the time, which I believe the vanilla theano backend is aside from padding, right?  But even without that, I'd prefer this.

> This is the 'clean' solution, but take a look at those layer classes, some of them are monsters already. I really don't feel that we should make things even more complicated by merging them into one.

Let me know what you think of the above alternative - less clean, but with very little code added to the library.

> That doesn't sound very elegant at all.

It's not, but theano is forcing us to be this way.

> I don't think it is, to be honest. There are plenty of other things that would be more useful to spend our time on.

I don't think it would take much time to implement something simple, although I'm not convinced the simple solution is best.

> We would essentially be doing double work as there are plans to fix this at the Theano level, which is where it should be fixed.

If we do wait for Theano, we need to be very careful about documenting this - i.e., rather than `raise ImportError("dnn not available")`, have the error message be something like "lasagne.layers.dnn was imported, but cudnn is not available.  The code you are running will only work on a computer with a GPU and with cudnn installed.  If you'd like to use this code on another system, you need to change all instances of Conv2DDNNLayer to Conv2DLayer and all instances of MaxPool2DDNNLayer to MaxPool2DLayer (etc etc)"

> it should not quietly go and use a different one if that one isn't available, it should fail.

It absolutely should not go quietly!  There should be a warning.  I don't think it should fail though.  A good example of this is the way `librosa` handles its dependency on `scikits.samplerate`; when `resample` is called and `scikits.samplerate` is not available, it will fall back on `scipy`'s resample (which is slower), but the user is warned about it.

To summarize, the reason this is frustrating to me is that as it is, Lasagne (by way of Theano) basically forces you to write non-portable code when you want to use one of the "better" backends.  But, with some relatively small changes to the codebase, we could make Lasagne code transparently (with error messages) portable up to the inherent deficiencies of each individual backend (with clear error messages in these cases).

Hey there - the only person who can get you an authoritative answer on this is @ltfish, who is the author of the CFG code. I can answer a few things though:

- enable_symbolic_back_traversal along with keep_state SHOULD have been enough to do that recovery correctly. This might be a bug. 
- CFG is a synonym for CFGAccurate
- CFGFast works by searching for known blocks of bytes which resemble function prologues, so it makes sense that it'd be able to find your functions but not any edges to them.

The problems seems to be the result of multiple side effects, so to sum up:
- When using multiple lifters, the postprocessors are called once per lifter on the block (lifting/__init__.py lift() is recursive)
- the pyvex/lifting/zerodivision.py and/or block merge of lift are messing up with the block on the second entry.

```python

The first instruction is not currently supported. You need an extra lifter. The following should work:
```python
import archinfo
from archinfo import Endness
import pyvex
import bitstring
from pyvex.lifting.util import *
from pyvex.lifting import register

class Instruction_MRS_TPIDR_EL1(Instruction):
    bin_format = '110101010011100011010000100ttttt'
    name = 'mrs_tpidr_el1'

    # linux uses tpidr_el1 to store my_cpu_offset
    # arch/arm64/include/asm/percpu.h
    # let's just return 0

    # documentation for instruction is on section D8.2.88 of
    # https://yurichev.com/mirrors/ARMv8-A_Architecture_Reference_Manual_(Issue_A.a).pdf

    def compute_result(self):
        zero = self.constant(0, Type.int_64)
        self.put(zero, 'x%d' % int(self.data['t'], base=2))

    def disassemble(self):
        return (self.addr,
                'MRS X%d, TPIDR_el1' % int(self.data['t'], base=2),
                [self.rawbits])

class LifterAarch64(GymratLifter):
    instrs = [Instruction_MRS_TPIDR_EL1]

register(LifterAarch64, 'AARCH64')
```

I partially solved the problem by:
- specifying the target_system at instantiation of a Vehicle object, 
- in MAVconnection, check that 
```(self.target_system == 0) or (self.target_system == msg.get_srcSystem()``` before calling the message listener functions

I could create a vehicle like this 
```
import dronekit
vehicle1 = dronekit.connect('127.0.0.1:14550', target_system=1)
```
And it only received messages from the drone with sysid 1, which is what we want, great!


However if then I create a second vehicle object:
```
vehicle2 = dronekit.connect('127.0.0.1:14550', target_system=2)
```
Then the link to the first drone is lost. This comes down to `mavutil.mavlink_connection`s who cannot share the same udp link.

Maybe the `connect` function should create a single MAVConnection object, but return a list of vehicles? I am happy to contribute to that feature, but I would like to be sure I go in the right direction.

The round-trip API call penalty is usually much steeper so I minimize paging unless there's a critical motivator. Another optimization in this case could be the use of a fields constraint. I don't recall offhand if this API supports it, but many GCP APIs use field constraints to speed up their back-end calls, so limiting fields to just the essentials could make this even snappier.

    python setup.py -v build_ext -i

> There is a circular issue in which setup.py can't run to work out which dependencies are needed in order they can be installed. setup.py needs to run without the dependencies so they can be installed.

> You're current version is breaking in python 3 (iteritems has been removed - I suspect it's safe to just use items which works in both and the list is probably not too painful to use explicitly in 2).

### static linking

Static linking has issues, the tests don't pass, openMP and static linking is a pain, see https://stackoverflow.com/questions/23869981/linking-openmp-statically-with-gcc So where do you want to support static linking? I made quite some effort to include static linking but now I doubt that it's worth it. In the end, you have to create a shared library `pyfftw.so` so we don't have a static include-it-all executable anyways. So why bother with static linking? Removing support for it would simplify the `setup.py`.

### package data

When do you use them? To create a release distribution for windows? I guess in that case it's ok to statically code up the libraries because you have control over the machine where you do the packaging. But is there no threading support on windows? For the moment, I left `get_package_data()` as it is.

### windows

I only tested on debian jessie and ubuntu 16.04. Could you please test the code on windows? I saw that you set up appveyor but on this pr appveyor is not run. I wonder about threads and MPI on windows. It looks like you never supported multiple threads on windows.

### quick test

In my opinion the tests weren't quick, so I commented out all but the first three tests but didn't commit this. Since you use subprocesses, it is not possible to quit all tests once they have been started with `Ctrl-C`, quite annoying.

Actually, I just tried, and seems that just changing [line 504 in cast.h](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/master/include/pybind11/cast.h#L504) from
```C++
    operator std::reference_wrapper<type>() { return std::ref(*((type *) this->value)); }
```
to
```C++
    operator std::reference_wrapper<type>() { return std::ref(static_cast<type&>(*this)); }
```
already does the job.

You're probably looking for an optional type. If you can use C++17, just use `std::optional` with [`#include <pybind11/stl.h>`](http://pybind11.readthedocs.io/en/master/advanced/cast/stl.html#c-17-library-containers). Otherwise, if you can use Boost, there's `boost::optional`; the code needed to bind it with pybind11 is in that same link. Otherwise, if you can find some other type that behaves like an optional, you can similarly expose it to pybind11. You may be able to use `std::experimental::optional` if neither option works for you.

#Make the header and write the file
def _write_lateral_file(energy, x, fitdata, z_bin_start):
    ## Make the header ##
    weplofBAMS=0.0 #TODO move somewhere else?
    t = datetime.datetime.now()
    now = datetime.datetime.fromtimestamp(time.mktime(t.timetuple())) 
    energystart = '$STOM' + '\n' #start columns of measurement for one energy
    header1 = '%VERSION 02' + '\n'
    header2 = '%DATE    ' + now.ctime() + '\n'
    header3 = '%BMTY PRO' + '\n'
    header4a = '%TYPE MeasuredSpotFluenceX' + '\n' #for x measurements
    header5a = '%AXIS X' + '\n' #for x measurements
    header4b = '%TYPE MeasuredSpotFluenceY' + '\n' #for y measurements
    header5b = '%AXIS Y' + '\n' #for y measurements
    header6 = '%ENERGY ' + str(energy)+'.0' + '\n'
    header7 = '%NET ' + str(weplofBAMS) + '\n' 
    header8 = '%ZPOS ' + str(-1*z_bin_start) + '\n' #Isocenter at ZPOS=0, positive axis towards nozzle (opposite the SHIELD-HIT setup) 
    energystop = '$ENOM' + '\n' #ends columns of measurement for one energy
    ## Write the lateral profile file for x ##
    lat_filename_x = output_path  + 'LateralX.w2cad'
    output_file = open(lat_filename_x, "a") #choose a-mode for open() in order to append
    output_file.write(energystart + header1 + header2 + header3 + header4a + header5a + header6 + header7 + header8)
    for i in range(len(fitdata)):
        if fitdata[i] > 0.1: #only write out data actually containing the profile
            output_file.write('<' + str(x[i])+ ' ' + '0.000 ' + '0.000 ' + str(fitdata[i])+ '>' + "\n")
    output_file.write(energystop)
    output_file.close()
    ## Write the lateral profile file for y (assuming perfect laterally symmetric beam, aka use same data) ##
    lat_filename_y = output_path  + 'LateralY.w2cad'
    output_file = open(lat_filename_y, "a") #choose a-mode for open() in order to append
    output_file.write(energystart + header1 + header2 + header3 + header4b + header5b + header6 + header7 + header8)
    for i in range(len(fitdata)):
        if fitdata[i] > 0.1: #only write out data actually containing the profile
            output_file.write('<' + '0.000 ' + str(x[i])+ ' ' + '0.000 ' + str(fitdata[i])+ '>' + "\n")
    output_file.write(energystop)
    output_file.close()
```

I think this can be accomplished by the sweep generator yielding a stateful object instead of just measurement numbers. What about doing somethings like this: 

(A class method on the Parameter class):
```python
def sweep(self, iterable):
  for value in iterable:
    self.set(iterable):
    yield self.instrument
```

In this case instrument is a stateful object. If we want to make a measurement dataset, we could make a measurement class which can be different for different measurement types. This measurement class takes as argument a generator which it will unrole when calling "run". The init method of this class can accept as argument a list of properties or methods which will enable it to extract measurement data from the stateful object for each time the generator yields the next value. For instance: 

```python
class Measurement:
  def init(self, properties): # and other useful info about the measurement, like data format
     self._properties = properties

  def run(self, generator): 
     for instrument in generator(): 
        for property in self._properties: 
            value = instrument.get(property)
            .... # code to store the property and value at this time. 
```

So the complete measurement looks like: 

```python
m = qc.Measurement(["gate1", "sd1"])
m.run(instrument.sweep(gate1=np.linspace(0, 1, 10)))
```
 I know this looks a bit like what we have already, but i think what is better about this design is that the run method can except any generator. Consider for example this: 

```python
m.run((instrument.set(value) for value in [0, 1, 2]))
```
Assuming that the set method returns "self". Another interesting thing you could do is perform non-linear sweeps. For example, finding the resonance frequency by stepping the frequency and iteratively finding the maximum value with newtons method: 

```python
def find_max_frequency(instrument, init):
  max_estimate = init
  while(True): # yield values until we have found the max
    .... # code to update the estimate of the max by measuring a local derivative. Adaptive stepping!
    yield instrument
``` 

My idea's have not matured, but consider how we could "zip" such a generator with a linear sweep to, for instance, flexibly program a measurement which finds a resonance at each gate voltage (for example).

OK, so the way the code works, it only actually commits changes that are in the index after the diff-index check. So using `--cached` is technically correct.

Now, should doctr be smarter about detecting changes after --command, in case they aren't added properly? Probably so. Requiring the command to add all files, not just new ones, to the index, is cumbersome and error prone (it is *only* necessary if the file changed isn't already changed by the docs sync). 

I believe this fixes my immediate issue, and as far as I know, SymPy is the only repo using --command (could be wrong about that, though). It shouldn't be a regression, though, because if the command is currently changing files that aren't added, it still doesn't commit them (and if no files actually are added, it errors). 

I opened https://github.com/drdoctr/doctr/issues/267 to track this.

Hi @junghoon-kang,

support for `I` and `I;16` in the `ToTensor` transform was added in [this PR](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/pull/122), the use case was for things like MRI images, where conversion from an int32 image to a float32 representation causes information to be lost. It's also important to note that calling `ToTensor` on `I` or `I;16` images will also do no rescaling of the range of the images. 

one work around, if you want float images is to explicitly call `.float` on the response, or convert your images after conversion to a pil image. These operations can also be made as a transform inside your `Compose`
we plan to add a memory efficient version soon via https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/4594

> BTW: anything special about cleaning up this memory?
> If I cleanup from JVM side will Python also let go of it through normal GC?

The ndarray in Python maintains a reference to the Java DirectNDArray through JNI so Java cannot collect anything until Python releases it. Whenever there are no more references to the ndarray in Python it will collect the ndarray which releases the DirectNDArray and then Java is free to collect it whenever it runs GC. So the only cleanup would be to make sure you aren't inadvertently keeping a reference to the ndarray longer than intended.
